food_name: Kangkung / Water Spinach (raw, leaves and tender stems)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has no specific entry for Ipomoea aquatica (water spinach / kangkung). The nearest parent concept is a generic leafy green vegetable. A new FoodOn concept is recommended for this major Southeast Asian vegetable.
ontology.foodex2_description: Water spinach / kangkung (Ipomoea aquatica), raw leaves and tender stems
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A14AB used as closest parent for Ipomoea aquatica raw; no exact FoodEx2 entry exists for this species.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for Ipomoea aquatica as a food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of raw kangkung is approximately 150g, equivalent to a generous handful of leaves and tender stems as typically prepared before stir-frying.

nutrient_source.title: Kangkung / Water Spinach (raw) — FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore food composition data
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table; Health Promotion Board (HPB), Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table for Ipomoea aquatica (raw), cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database. Both are approved primary regional sources. Values are consistent across both sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of non-starchy leafy green vegetables — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for raw leafy green vegetables (spinach, lettuce, watercress); GI estimated at approximately 15 for Ipomoea aquatica raw
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Ipomoea aquatica (kangkung) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at approximately 15 (low) by analogy with comparable raw non-starchy leafy green vegetables (spinach GI ~15, watercress GI ~15, lettuce GI ~15) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150g serving = (15 × 3.1 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.70, rounded to 0.7. This is negligible and consistent with the very low carbohydrate content of this vegetable.

hypertension.key_factor: High potassium (312mg/100g), good magnesium (71mg/100g), moderate natural sodium (113mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Kangkung is a beneficial vegetable for hypertension management due to its high potassium content (312mg/100g) which supports blood pressure regulation; the moderate natural sodium (113mg/100g) is inherent to the plant and not added salt, and is not a concern at typical serving sizes of 150g raw.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low calorie density (19 kcal/100g), low digestible carbohydrate (3.1g/100g), low glycemic index (~15), high dietary fibre (2.1g/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Raw kangkung has a very low glycemic index (~15) and negligible glycemic load per serving (GL ~0.7), making it an excellent vegetable choice for blood glucose management in Type 2 Diabetes; its high fibre content further slows glucose absorption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.0g/100g), high potassium (312mg/100g), high vitamin C (55mg/100g), high vitamin A as beta-carotene (315mcg RAE/100g), zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kangkung is cardioprotective: it contains zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat, and is rich in potassium (312mg/100g), vitamin C (55mg/100g), and beta-carotene (315mcg RAE/100g), all of which support vascular health and reduce oxidative stress associated with cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High vitamin C (55mg/100g) for collagen synthesis and antioxidant activity; good vitamin A (315mcg RAE/100g); magnesium (71mg/100g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high vitamin C content (55mg/100g) in kangkung supports collagen synthesis and provides antioxidant protection relevant to joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; magnesium (71mg/100g) additionally supports muscle and nerve function.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kangkung (Ipomoea aquatica), also known as water spinach or morning glory, is the most commonly consumed leafy green vegetable in Singapore hawker cooking and across Southeast Asia. It is a semi-aquatic plant native to Southeast Asia and South China, characterised by hollow arrow-shaped leaves and tender hollow stems. The plant grows in or near water, which accounts for its high water content (approximately 92%) and its slightly elevated natural sodium (113mg/100g). Kangkung is extremely cheap and widely available at Singapore wet markets year-round. Nutritionally, it is very low in calories (19 kcal/100g) and digestible carbohydrates (3.1g/100g), making it suitable for calorie-controlled and diabetic diets. It is rich in vitamin C (55mg/100g), vitamin A as beta-carotene (315mcg RAE/100g), folate (57mcg/100g), potassium (312mg/100g), calcium (77mg/100g), magnesium (71mg/100g), and iron (1.7mg/100g). The most iconic Singapore and Malaysian preparation is kangkung belacan — stir-fried with sambal, shrimp paste (belacan), and fresh chilli — a hawker staple found at virtually every zi char stall. It also appears in char kway teow, Assam laksa, and Vietnamese soups (rau muống). The raw vegetable has a mild, slightly grassy flavour with a crisp-tender texture in the stems and soft leaves. Clinically, kangkung is beneficial for hypertension (high potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (very low GI ~15, low GL), and cardiovascular health (zero cholesterol, negligible saturated fat, antioxidant vitamins). It is vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Regional Food Composition Table and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo database, both approved primary regional sources. The food is a well-documented raw vegetable with consistent values across multiple approved sources. GI is estimated by analogy with comparable non-starchy leafy greens from the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source.
notes: Saturated fat (0.0g/100g) and cholesterol (0.0g/100g) are confirmed zero per FAO/INFOODS SEA and HPB Singapore data for raw Ipomoea aquatica — these are naturally zero for this non-animal leafy green vegetable. Vitamin B12 (0.0mcg/100g) and vitamin D (0.0mcg/100g) are naturally zero as this is a plant food with no animal-derived components. Trans fat (0.0g/100g) is naturally zero for this raw unprocessed vegetable. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat set to -1.0: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ipomoea aquatica raw is not reported in FAO/INFOODS SEA or HPB Singapore database entries consulted. Water content estimated at 92.0g/100g consistent with FAO/INFOODS SEA data. GI value of approximately 15 is an analogous estimate based on comparable raw non-starchy leafy green vegetables (spinach, watercress, lettuce) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for Ipomoea aquatica is available in the approved GI source. Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as (15 × 3.1 × 1.5) / 100 = 0.70. The sodium content of 113mg/100g is natural plant sodium from the semi-aquatic growing environment and is not added salt. FoodOn URI is null: no specific FoodOn concept exists for Ipomoea aquatica; closest parent is generic leafy green vegetable. SNOMED CT code is null: no food-item SNOMED CT concept identified for this species.
